text: This video will illustrate a robotic thymectomy through the right chest in a 45 year old gentleman.
Here is seen that white structure is the phrenic nerve on the right side.
Dissection is started out by first mobilizing the inferior poles of the thymus gland.
This operation is typically done through total four small incisions.
The thymic tissue is completely mobilized off of the pericardium, which can be seen below.
Instruments that I typically use in the operation are simply just the Cartier and the monopolar spatula.
pleura on the anterior aspect of the mediastinum is mobilized.
And we take this is section all the way up cephalad.
We routinely enter the contralateral pleura as well as seen here.
There seen in view now is the left lung that's inflating and deflating.
The inferior pole from the left side is then also taken down and completely mobilized.
Care is taken to avoid any injury to the contralateral phrenic nerve.
And we're looking for it there and more than likely it is inferior to where we were dissecting.
This dissection is fairly avascular as long as you're in the right plane.
And again, it's taken all the way in cephalad to the level of the innominate vein.
And from time to time, you'll see me fold that fat over to make sure that we don't get the phrenic nerve or injury in any way.
Here again, you can see the phrenic nerve on the right side, that white structure at the bottom of the screen.
We take care to stay away from it as much as possible and just score the overlying pleura here.
And once that's done, we again, stay on the pericardium and mark our way up towards the innominate vein, which could be seen right there, that dark blue structure.
I think that the approach from the right side
is slightly easier than the left because the innominate vein in my mind is easier to find.
You can trace the superior vena cava as well as the internal mammary veins and typically you can see it at the junction there.
And here, care is taken to slowly dissect the thymic tissue off of the innominate vein.
And you'll see here, shortly in view, will be the superior horn of the thymus gland, which will be slowly delivered into the surgical field, as you can see here.
And that is a delivery of the right thymic horn.
And again, staying on the innominate vein and dissecting it over to the left side, you'll see the other horn of the thymus gland, which will be slowly delivered into the surgical field.
Oftentimes, there will be small perforating vessels, which could either be ligated with clips, or if they're small enough, cauterized.
And you can see here the thymic horn slowly being delivered into the field.
The remainder of the thymus gland is immobilized and is removed through a small endo-catch bag.
This patient did very well post-operatively.
He was discharged home on post-operative day one and recovered without any incident.
